goal: Review the proof and issues for rm-02063; agree on the key remaining gaps and an action plan.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Let me cut to the heart of this before we overcomplicate it. The center of mixability is forced by a one-line linear argument: if $X_1+\cdots+X_n = n\mu$ a.s. with each $X_i \sim F$, then take expectations of both sides — *provided* $F$ has a finite mean. We get $n\mathbb{E}[X_1] = n\mu$, so $\mu = \mathbb{E}_F[X]$. The center is the mean, full stop, and it's unique. So for distributions with finite first moment, $\mathcal{M}_n(\mu)\cap\mathcal{M}_n(\nu)=\varnothing$ whenever $\mu\ne\nu$.

The whole problem therefore lives or dies on the **moment hypothesis**. The interesting case — and I'd bet this is where the claimed proof has a gap — is heavy-tailed $F$ with no finite mean, or undefined mean (Cauchy-type). There you cannot just "take expectations," because the linearity of expectation argument is invalid.

So my questions for the room:
1. Does the problem implicitly assume finite mean? If so, this is trivial and we should say so.
2. If not, can we construct a *counterexample* with an $F$ of undefined mean that sits in two different $\mathcal{M}_n(\mu)$? Think Cauchy: a sum of $n$ i.i.d. Cauchy$(0,\gamma)$ has the same shape as a single Cauchy scaled by $n$ — that's the right place to look for nonuniqueness, because the "center" is then a location parameter not pinned by an expectation.
3. Even without finite mean, symmetry might pin down a center via the *median* or via characteristic functions: $\hat F(t)^{?}$ constraints from $\sum X_i = n\mu$ a.s. give $|\hat F(t)|=1$ on a set, which forces lattice-type rigidity.

I want to see exactly which moment assumption the existing proof used. Show me the line where they invoked $\mathbb{E}[X]$, and tell me whether $F$ was assumed integrable. That's the gap.

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan, you've correctly isolated the live question, but let me sharpen the characteristic-function approach you gestured at in point 3 — it actually resolves the Cauchy worry against you.

Suppose $X_1+\cdots+X_n = n\mu$ a.s. with each $X_i\sim F$. Condition on $X_2,\dots,X_n$: then $X_1$ equals a constant $n\mu - (X_2+\cdots+X_n)$ a.s. — but $X_1$ has distribution $F$, which need not be degenerate, so we cannot localize that way. Instead work with the joint characteristic function. The constraint $\sum X_i = n\mu$ a.s. means the joint distribution of $(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ is supported on the hyperplane $\sum x_i = n\mu$. Evaluate the characteristic function along the diagonal direction $t(1,\dots,1)$:
$$\mathbb{E}\,e^{it\sum X_j} = e^{itn\mu}, \quad\text{so } |\mathbb{E}\,e^{it\sum X_j}|=1 \text{ for all } t.$$
This is the rigidity I want. Now suppose the *same* $F$ also satisfies $\sum Y_j = n\nu$ a.s. for a possibly different coupling. The single-coordinate marginal $\hat F$ is fixed; what differs is only the coupling.

Here is the clean argument that pins the center, valid **without any moment assumption**. Take the symmetric truncation: let $g(t) = \arg$ of the characteristic function is not well-defined globally, so instead use $F$'s own symmetrization. Consider $Z = X_1 - X_1'$ with $X_1'$ an independent copy; $Z$ is symmetric and always has a center at $0$. The mixability constraints for $\mu$ and $\nu$ both descend to the symmetrization, and the *difference* coupling $\sum(X_j - X_j') = n(\mu-\nu)$... no — both give $0$, so symmetrization loses the center entirely. That tells me the center genuinely lives in the *location* data, exactly your Cauchy intuition.

So the decisive test is the Cauchy case directly. For Cauchy$(0,\gamma)$, $\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ with i.i.d. copies is Cauchy$(0,n\gamma)$ — **not** a constant — so the i.i.d. coupling never lies in any $\mathcal{M}_n(\mu)$. Complete mixability requires a *dependent* coupling forcing the sum to be deterministic, and Cauchy with its log-concave-failing, slowly decaying tails cannot be made to sum to a constant at all: the tail of $\sum X_i$ under any coupling dominates the tail of a single $X_i$ (by a Fréchet–Hoeffding / comonotone lower-bound argument on the worst tail), which cannot be a point mass. So Cauchy is simply **not** $n$-completely mixable — it is not a counterexample, it is a non-member.

This reframes the whole problem the way I like: the question is really about the *structure of the set of mixable $F$*, and within that set whether $\mu$ is an invariant. My claim: the right invariant is a **trimmed/principal-value mean** that exists precisely on the mixable cone. Concretely, $n$-complete mixability forces $F$ to have controlled tails (the support-rearrangement obstruction of Wang–Wang is exactly a tail-balance condition), and on that cone the principal value

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Good — Laci has essentially closed the door on the Cauchy escape, and I think he's right, but let me say *why* he's right in a way that makes the theorem transparent. The key is his observation that the joint distribution sits on the hyperplane $\sum x_i = n\mu$. Now here is the clean tail argument, which is really a comonotone lower bound — the same Fréchet–Hoeffding philosophy I use when counting integer points on quadrics with Duke and Rudnick.

Claim: **if $F$ is $n$-completely mixable, then $F$ has a finite mean.** Suppose $\sum_{i=1}^n X_i = n\mu$ a.s. Then for each $i$,
$$ X_i = n\mu - \sum_{j\ne i} X_j. $$
So $X_i$ is bounded above by $n\mu - (n-1)\,\mathrm{ess\,inf}\, F$ and below by $n\mu - (n-1)\,\mathrm{ess\,sup}\,F$ — no, that needs boundedness. Let me instead do it honestly with tails. The right statement is: complete mixability forces $F$ to have a finite mean. Take expectations is *not* allowed a priori, but the constraint gives, for the upper tail, $\mathbb{P}(X_1 > t) \le \mathbb{P}(\sum_{j\ge 2} X_j < n\mu - t) \le (n-1)\,\mathbb{P}(X_2 < (n\mu-t)/(n-1))$ by a union bound — and symmetrically for the lower tail. Iterating, the surviving tail must decay fast enough that $\int |x|\,dF < \infty$. A distribution with no first moment, like Cauchy, simply violates this — exactly Laci's conclusion.

Once finite mean is forced, Dan's one-line argument is not a *hypothesis* but a *theorem*: integrability is automatic on the mixable cone, $\mu = \mathbb{E}_F[X]$, and uniqueness follows. So the structure is:

1. Mixability $\Rightarrow$ finite mean (tail/comonotone rigidity — this is the real content);
2. Finite mean $\Rightarrow$ $\mu = \mathbb{E}_F[X]$ (linearity — trivial).

The theorem behind the technical one is step (1): **the mixable cone is contained in $L^1$.** That's where Laci's "trimmed mean" intuition is actually unnecessary — there's no need for a principal value, because heavy tails are simply expelled from the cone. The action item is to nail the tail iteration rigorously and confirm it forces $L^1$ for every fixed $n\ge 2$. I'd bet the original proof's gap is precisely that it asserted finite mean rather than *deriving* it.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Peter, I love where this is going, but let me stress-test step (1), because I think the union-bound tail argument as written has a sign/quantifier bug that we need to fix before we declare victory.

You wrote $\mathbb{P}(X_1 > t) \le \mathbb{P}(\sum_{j\ge 2} X_j < n\mu - t)$. That's correct from the hyperplane constraint. But then bounding $\mathbb{P}(\sum_{j\ge 2}X_j < n\mu-t)$ by $(n-1)\mathbb{P}(X_2 < (n\mu-t)/(n-1))$ is the *wrong direction* of a union bound — the event "sum is small" is not contained in "some term is small"; it's contained in "*all* terms aren't large," which is an intersection, not a union. The correct statement is $\{\sum_{j\ge2} X_j < s\} \subseteq \bigcup_{j\ge 2}\{X_j < s/(n-1)\}$, which gives exactly your inequality — so it *is* a union bound and you're fine. I retract the bug; let me instead point at the real subtlety.

The real subtlety is whether the iteration *closes*. Define the upper-tail function $\bar F(t)=\mathbb P(X_1>t)$. Your inequality reads
$$\bar F(t) \le (n-1)\, F\!\Big(\frac{n\mu - t}{n-1}\Big),$$
i.e. the *upper* tail at $t$ is controlled by the *lower* tail at a point that goes to $-\infty$ as $t\to+\infty$. So this couples upper and lower tails, and the iteration only forces integrability if the two tails feed each other into geometric decay. For a *symmetric* heavy-tailed $F$ this is clean and you get the contradiction; for a one-sidedly heavy $F$ I want to see the coupled recursion written out. So my concrete ask:

1. Write the recursion as a map on the pair $(\bar F, F_{\text{lower}})$ and show its only fixed points decaying slower than $1/t$ are killed — that's the actual content, and it's a contraction-type argument, very much in the spirit of a barrier/potential function: define $\Phi(t)=t\cdot(\bar F(t)+F_{\text{lower}}(-t))$ and show the constraint forces $\Phi(t)\to 0$.
2. **Here's the cleaner route I'd bet on, and it sidesteps tails entirely.** Use Laci's characteristic-function rigidity *constructively*. The joint c.f. on the diagonal satisfies $|\hat F(t)|^? $— more precisely, supported on $\sum x_i = n\mu$ means $\prod$-type constraints fail because of dependence, but the marginal $\hat F$ is fixed. Now the comonotone coupling gives a Fréchet–Hoeffding lower bound on the variance of $\sum X_i$: for *any* coupling, $\mathrm{Var}(\sum X_i)\ge$ (sum of comonotone correlations), but more usefully, a deterministic sum needs $\sum X_i$ constant, and the minimal achievable $L^2$ spread of $\sum X_i$ over all couplings with fixed marginal $F$ is governed by the *mean* of $F$. If $F\notin L^1$, that minimal spread is infinite, so you can never reach a constant. That's a one-line $L^2$/optimal-transport obstruction rather than a delicate tail iteration.

So I agree with Peter's two-

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan, your $L^2$ shortcut is appealing but it has a fatal gap: if $F\notin L^2$ but $F\in L^1$, the "minimal spread is infinite" claim fails to distinguish the cases you care about — and worse, the optimal-transport spread you invoke is the *variance*, which says nothing when the second moment is infinite but the first is finite. The clean obstruction is finite mean, not finite variance, so we must run the $L^1$ tail argument, not the $L^2$ one. Let me settle step (1) honestly, because Peter's recursion does close — but the clean way is to symmetrize the constraint, not to chase the coupled tail pair.

Here is the decisive observation. Mixability is preserved under a translation: $F\in\mathcal M_n(\mu)$ iff the shifted law $F(\cdot+\mu)\in\mathcal M_n(0)$. So WLOG $\mu=0$ and $\sum_{i=1}^n X_i = 0$ a.s. Now take an independent copy $(X_1',\dots,X_n')$ of the *same coupling*; then $\sum_i (X_i - X_i') = 0$ a.s. too, with each $Z_i := X_i - X_i'$ symmetric, $Z_i\sim G$ where $G$ is the symmetrization of $F$. So $G$ is $n$-completely mixable with center $0$, and crucially **$F\in L^1 \iff G\in L^1$** (symmetrization preserves the $1$-integrability). For the symmetric $G$, Peter's coupled recursion *decouples*: writing $\bar G(t)=\mathbb P(Z_1>t)$, the hyperplane bound gives
$$\bar G(t)\le (n-1)\,\bar G\!\Big(\tfrac{t}{n-1}\Big)\quad\text{is the wrong direction; the right one is } \bar G(t)\le (n-1)\,G\big(-\tfrac{t}{n-1}\big)=(n-1)\bar G\big(\tfrac{t}{n-1}\big),$$
using symmetry $G(-s)=\bar G(s)$. This is now a *single* self-referential inequality, and it forces
$$\bar G(t)\le (n-1)\,\bar G\!\Big(\tfrac{t}{n-1}\Big).$$
Iterating $k$ times, $\bar G(t)\le (n-1)^k \bar G(t/(n-1)^k)$ — which alone does **not** decay, so the union bound in this naive form is too lossy. The genuine content (Wang–Wang, *"The complete mixability and convex minimization problems with monotone marginal densities"*) is that complete mixability requires the **mean condition**: the support must allow a balanced rearrangement, and the precise tail obstruction comes from the fact that the comonotone *lower* bound on the sum's spread is finite iff $\mathbb E|X|<\infty$.

So I now agree with Peter's two-step skeleton, but the rigorous engine is not a fixed-point contraction on tails — it is the **Fréchet–Hoeffding rearrangement obstruction in $L^1$**:
- A constant sum $\sum X_i=0$ means the joint law is the *most negatively dependent* possible in the diagonal direction; the existence of such a coupling with fixed marginal $F$ is exactly Wang–Wang's mixability criterion, and that criterion is vacuous unless $\int|x|\,dF<\infty$.
- Given $F\in L^1$, integr

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Laci, your symmetrization is the right move — it's exactly the device I use when passing from a Maass form to its microlocal lift: kill the location, study the spectrum. But notice what symmetrization *costs* us: it converts the question about the center $\mu$ into a pure integrability question about $G$, and then the center uniqueness is recovered *afterward* by Dan's linearity. So the logical skeleton is now clean. The one honest gap remaining is that the naive iteration $\bar G(t)\le (n-1)\bar G(t/(n-1))$ is, as you correctly flag, **too lossy** — it's consistent with $\bar G(t)\sim t^{-\alpha}$ for *any* $\alpha>0$, since $(n-1)\cdot (t/(n-1))^{-\alpha} = (n-1)^{1+\alpha} t^{-\alpha} \gg t^{-\alpha}$. The union bound throws away the dependence structure that is the whole point.

Here is what I think is the *real* theorem, and it's not a tail iteration at all — it's a positivity/duality statement, in the spirit of the explicit formula where one tests against a positive function. Complete mixability with center $0$ says: there is a coupling with $\sum X_i \equiv 0$. Test against any **bounded, increasing, odd** function $\psi$:
$$0 = \mathbb{E}\,\psi\!\Big(\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i X_i\Big),$$
and by a Chebyshev/rearrangement (Hardy–Littlewood) inequality the comonotone coupling *maximizes* $\mathbb{E}[\psi(\bar X)\cdot \mathrm{sgn}]$ — so the existence of the *vanishing* coupling forces a two-sided balance: $\int_0^\infty \bar F(t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^0 F(t)\,dt$, and **both must be finite**. That last finiteness is exactly $F\in L^1$, and it is *not* derivable from a single-tail recursion; it requires the Fréchet–Hoeffding *upper* bound (comonotone), which is the dual constraint Dan reached for and Laci correctly anchored in Wang–Wang.

So my proposed action plan, sharpened:
1. Drop the tail-iteration approach — it genuinely doesn't close, you both showed that.
2. Run the **Hardy–Littlewood rearrangement obstruction**: mixability $\Rightarrow$ the comonotone coupling's diagonal spread is finite $\iff F\in L^1$. This is the real content of step (1), and it is Wang–Wang's criterion read correctly.
3. Then Dan's linearity gives $\mu=\mathbb{E}_F[X]$, uniqueness, done.

But let me ask the provocative question, because I always want the theorem behind the theorem: **is uniqueness of the center really an $L^1$ phenomenon, or is it a convexity phenomenon?** I suspect the cleanest statement is that the map $\mu\mapsto \mathcal{M}_n(\mu)$ is a *translate* — $\mathcal{M}_n(\mu) = \mu + \mathcal{M}_n(0)$ — and two translates of a fixed set in distribution-space intersect iff the centers coincide, *because* the linear functional "mean" separates them. That makes uniqueness automatic and the only content is that the mean is well-defined on the cone. That's the theorem. Everything else is plumbing.

messages.author: coordinator
messages.body:
**Action plan synthesized** (7 steps):

Prove uniqueness of the mixability center by showing the n-completely mixable cone lies in L^1 (via a Hardy-Littlewood/Fréchet-Hoeffding rearrangement obstruction rather than a lossy tail iteration), after which linearity of expectation forces the center to equal the mean and gives uniqueness.

plan.summary: Prove uniqueness of the mixability center by showing the n-completely mixable cone lies in L^1 (via a Hardy-Littlewood/Fréchet-Hoeffding rearrangement obstruction rather than a lossy tail iteration), after which linearity of expectation forces the center to equal the mean and gives uniqueness.

steps.title: Establish trivial case under finite mean
steps.body: Formally prove that if F has a finite first moment and X_1+...+X_n = n*mu a.s. with each X_i ~ F, then taking expectations yields mu = E_F[X], hence the center is the unique mean. Conclude that M_n(mu) ∩ M_n(nu) = ∅ for mu ≠ nu among integrable F. This isolates the entire problem onto the moment hypothesis.

steps.title: Reduce to centered and symmetrized problem
steps.body: Prove the translation invariance F ∈ M_n(mu) iff F(·+mu) ∈ M_n(0), reducing WLOG to mu = 0 with sum X_i = 0 a.s. Then introduce the symmetrization G via Z_i = X_i - X_i' (independent copy of the coupling), showing G is n-completely mixable with center 0 and that F ∈ L^1 iff G ∈ L^1. This sets up the integrability question cleanly.

steps.title: Document the failure of the naive tail iteration
steps.body: Make explicit that the union-bound recursion barG(t) ≤ (n-1)·barG(t/(n-1)) is too lossy: it is consistent with barG(t) ~ t^{-alpha} for any alpha>0, since (n-1)·(t/(n-1))^{-alpha} = (n-1)^{1+alpha} t^{-alpha} >> t^{-alpha}. Confirm this approach cannot force L^1 and should be abandoned, so the team does not waste effort on a non-closing contraction.

steps.title: Prove mixability implies finite mean via rearrangement obstruction
steps.body: Carry out the Hardy-Littlewood / Fréchet-Hoeffding argument: a vanishing coupling sum_i X_i ≡ 0 forces a two-sided tail balance ∫_0^∞ barF(t) dt = ∫_{-∞}^0 F(t) dt with both integrals finite, which is exactly F ∈ L^1. Anchor this in the Wang-Wang complete mixability criterion (monotone marginal densities), showing the comonotone (most negatively dependent in the diagonal) coupling has finite diagonal spread iff F ∈ L^1, so heavy-tailed F is expelled from the cone.

steps.title: Confirm Cauchy / heavy-tailed F is a non-member, not a counterexample
steps.body: Verify rigorously that distributions with undefined or infinite mean (e.g. Cauchy(0,gamma)) are not n-completely mixable for any center: the i.i.d. sum is Cauchy(0,n*gamma) (never constant) and no coupling can make the deterministic-sum constraint hold given the L^1 obstruction from step 3. This closes the 'Cauchy escape' Dan raised and validates Laci's non-member claim.

steps.title: Assemble full uniqueness proof
steps.body: Combine the steps into the final logical skeleton: (1) mixability ⇒ F ∈ L^1 (rearrangement obstruction), (2) L^1 ⇒ mu = E_F[X] (linearity), hence the center is unique and M_n(mu) ∩ M_n(nu) = ∅ for mu ≠ nu with no moment hypothesis assumed. Optionally state the structural reformulation M_n(mu) = mu + M_n(0), with the mean functional separating translates.

steps.title: Verify each inequality and cite the literature precisely
steps.body: Independently check the direction of every inequality (especially the hyperplane/union-bound directions flagged in discussion), the symmetrization integrability equivalence, and the exact statement and applicability of the Wang-Wang mixability criterion and the Hardy-Littlewood rearrangement inequality. Confirm no hidden finite-variance assumption sneaks in (Dan's rejected L^2 shortcut must not be used).
